questions.question: Explain single-level directory system and mention its drawbacks.
keyPoints: Single-level has one directory containing all files
Simple to implement
Major drawback: naming conflicts - all files must have unique names
No grouping of related files
Difficult to manage as file count grows

questions.question: What are the advantages of tree-structured directory over two-level directory?
keyPoints: Hierarchical organization with unlimited nesting
Files can be grouped logically by type, project, or any criteria
Users can create personal subdirectories
Efficient search through branch-and-bound approach
Supports natural file organization mimicking real-world folders

questions.question: Differentiate between absolute path and relative path with examples.
keyPoints: Absolute path starts from root directory (/home/user/docs/file.txt)
Relative path starts from current working directory (docs/file.txt)
Absolute paths work from any location
Relative paths are shorter but depend on current directory
cd command changes current working directory

questions.question: Describe the hash table directory implementation and its advantages.
keyPoints: Uses hash function to compute index for file entries
Provides O(1) average-case search time
Reduces directory search overhead significantly
Requires handling hash collisions (chaining or open addressing)
More complex than linear list but much faster for large directories

questions.question: What are the basic operations that can be performed on a directory?
keyPoints: Create: Add new directory entry
Delete/Remove: Remove directory entry
Rename: Change file name
List: Display directory contents
Search: Find file by name
Open/Close: Access file for I/O operations

questions.question: Explain the concept of mounting in file systems with an example.
keyPoints: Mounting attaches a file system to directory tree
Mount point is an existing directory where new system attaches
After mounting, files become accessible through mount point path
Example: mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/usb attaches USB to /mnt/usb
Enables multiple file systems to be integrated into single hierarchy

questions.question: What is the difference between acyclic graph and general graph directory structures?
keyPoints: Acyclic graph has no cycles - directed without circular paths
General graph may contain cycles leading to infinite loops
Acyclic allows safe traversal without visited tracking
General graph needs cycle detection algorithms
Acyclic supports sharing without data inconsistency risks

questions.question: Explain the directory implementation using linear list and hash table. Compare their performance for search, insert, and delete operations.
keyPoints: Linear list: stores entries in array/list, O(n) search, O(1) insert at end, O(n) delete (requires shifting)
Hash table: uses hash function, O(1) average search, O(1) average insert/delete with collision handling
Linear list simple to implement, hash table faster but needs collision resolution
For small directories linear list may suffice, for large directories hash table preferred
Trade-off between simplicity (linear) and performance (hash)

questions.question: Describe different directory structures in detail with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Single-level: simple, naming conflicts - disadv
Two-level: MFD + UFD, user isolation, no subdirectories - disadv
Tree-structured: hierarchical, efficient grouping, no sharing - disadv
Acyclic graph: shared files/subdirs, cycle prevention needed - adv
General graph: maximum flexibility, cycle risk - disadv
Each suited to different use cases and OS requirements

questions.question: Explain how file metadata is stored in directory entries. Discuss the role of inode and FAT in directory implementation.
keyPoints: Directory entries store: file name, metadata pointer (inode number/FAT entry)
Inode: Unix metadata structure with permissions, timestamps, size, block pointers
FAT: Linked list of cluster numbers stored in table at volume start
Inode provides direct/indirect block addressing for fast file access
FAT simpler but slower due to sequential cluster traversal
Both provide mapping from file name to actual data locations
